summary: I was born in Bale Robe, which is located in the Oromia region of Ethiopia, back in the 1980s. Growing up there, I experienced firsthand the political oppression under Ethiopia's dictatorship. Bale Robe is where my story begins before I left for Kenya after high school to escape potential political kidnapping.

Oromia Region is where Bale Robe is located, and it’s the part of Ethiopia where I was born and raised. The political climate there was very harsh, with no freedom of speech allowed under the dictatorship. This environment influenced my decision to leave Ethiopia after high school to seek safety and freedom elsewhere.

Ethiopia is my homeland, where I was born and grew up during a time of political dictatorship and oppression. The government did not allow freedom of speech, and there was a real danger of being kidnapped for political reasons or tribal conflicts. I left Ethiopia in 1997 right after graduating high school to escape these threats and seek a safer life.

After leaving Ethiopia in 1997, I moved alone to Kenya and lived in refugee camps near Nairobi for over five years. Kenya was more stable than Ethiopia, despite some corruption, and it was there that I met my wife. We got married in Nairobi in 2000 before she moved to the United States, and I later joined her after applying for a marriage visa.

The United States became my new home in 2003 after I joined my wife who had resettled there with her family. I first lived in Minnesota, where I experienced culture shock and harsh winters, but also opportunities for a better future. Despite facing racism, I pursued a career in the medical field and worked hard to provide for my family and build a new life here.

Minnesota is where I settled when I first arrived in the United States in 2003 to be with my wife. The climate was a big shock to me, especially the bitter cold winters, very different from Nairobi. I started working in manufacturing but later moved into healthcare, taking classes for years to pursue nursing, all while raising my family here.

Some of my old friends moved to Las Vegas when they came to the United States, and they were much more surprised by the culture than I was. While I settled in Minnesota, I remember how different Las Vegas was from Nairobi and how my friends struggled to adjust. It showed me how diverse the American experience can be depending on where you live.
